data.title: Correction to Elliott Management Builds Stake in Barrick Headline
data.description:
Elliott Management built up a stake in Barrick Mining. "Elliot Management Builds Stake in Barrick, Encouraged by Breakup Prospects, Source Says," at 12:40 p.m. ET, misstated the activist investor's name in the headline.


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1319ET

Nymex Overview : Diesel Futures Slide on Profit-Taking After Recent Rally -- OPIS
data.description:
U.S. diesel contracts erased initial gains to fall sharply around midday Thursday, as investors opted to lock in profits following their recent sharp gains driven by Russian supply concerns amid Ukrainian attacks and tough U.S. sanctions.

At 11:50 a.m. ET, more-active January ULSD futures were off by 7.1cts to around $2.481/gal and December ULSD was 7.25cts lower to $2.565/gal.

Diesel contracts are on track to finish lower for a second session after an almost 15ct/gal hike during a three-day rally.

The U.S. Treasury Department said on Monday the Trump administration's sanctions against Russian oil companies Rosneft and Lukoil are already crimping Moscow's oil revenues and reducing the country's petroleum sales.

The sanctions, which were among the strongest imposed by the U.S. since the Ukraine war started in February 2022, set a Friday deadline for other companies to cut their dealings with the two Russian oil majors.

January RBOB futures were down by 2.45cts to $1.836/gal and December RBOB was 2.7cts lower to $1.905/gal.

January WTI crude contracts were 30cts lower to about $58.95/bbl, and the expiring December WTI futures were off by 5cts to $59.40/bbl.

London-based January ICE Brent was 15cts lower to around $63.35/bbl and February Brent was down by 25cts to $62.75/bbl.

In the spot market, Chicago, Group 3 and Los Angeles ULSD prices were down 10-13cts/gal, while other cash products largely tracked NYMEX futures movements.


This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.


--Reporting by Frank Tang, ftang@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1301ET

data.title: ASML launches technical academy in Phoenix to train in-demand engineers
Toronto Stocks Slip, Led Lower by Miners
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


Shares on Canada's main exchange retreated Thursday, dragged lower my weakness in mining stocks.

After beginning the session in positive territory, the Toronto Stock Exchange's S&P/TSX Composite Index by midday was 0.8% lower, at 30052.85. The mining sector was the biggest drag, leading broad losses and offsetting a gain by the finance segment.

The blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was 0.5% weaker, at 1773.52.

Among mining companies, Agnico Eagle was down 3.5%, Barrick Mining lost 3.9%, Hudbay Minerals shed 3.7% and Teck Resources shed 2.3%.


Other market movers:

Shares of Vizsla Silver dropped 13%, to 5.74 Canadian dollars (US$4.09), after the company priced a US$250 million offering of convertible notes, raising funds to develop its flagship project in Mexico and to prepare for possible future acquisitions.

Real Matters rose 1.5%, to C$6.11, after the company reported a slight increase in fourth-quarter revenue as growth in Canada and U.S. refinance-origination revenue offset lower activity in the U.S. purchase market.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1259ET

data.title: Elliot Management Builds Stake in Barrick, Encouraged by Breakup Prospects, Source Says
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


Activist investor Elliott Management has built up a sizable stake in Barrick Mining, putting pressure on the gold and copper producer to consider splitting up its operations.

The stake positions Elliott as one of Barrick's top 10 shareholders, a person familiar with the matter said, and was made because the activist was encouraged by the prospect of Barrick exploring a breakup.

A spokeswoman for the Toronto company declined to comment on the investment or Elliott's ambitions, saying the company doesn't comment on matters relating to individual shareholders.

Barrick has a clear plan and industry-leading assets, as well as a strengthened leadership team aligned on delivering strategic priorities, the spokeswoman said.

Other news outlets reported on Elliott's stake earlier this week.

Barrick is currently undergoing a review of operations, which is aimed at improving efficiencies and utilization across its assets. The plan, launched last month, involves taking a closer look at the Nevada Gold Mines operations and the Pueblo Viejo mine in Dominican Republic, both of which are minority owned by Newmont and account for more than half of Barrick's gold production.

The fresh emphasis on North America came shortly after Barrick parted ways with Mark Bristow, who had been chief executive for nearly seven years since the company bought Randgold Resources, an Africa-focused miner founded and run by Bristow for more than two decades. Company veteran Mark Hill was elevated to interim CEO.

Barrick's share price has more than doubled in 2025, and has risen more than 50% in the last three months alone as the company and other gold producers have benefited from a boost in the price of the precious metal to record highs because of investor demand for perceived safe-haven assets and central-bank purchases.

Still, it has lagged the jump seen in the share prices of some rivals, such as Canada's Agnico Eagle, whose steady production saw it vault Barrick this year to become second in world gold production behind Newmont.

Elliott has targeted other mining companies in the past.

In 2017, the firm took aim at BHP Group, which at the time lagged behind its peers during a commodity-price upswing. It urged the company, then known as BHP Billiton, to spin off its U.S. petroleum assets and unify a dual Sydney-London listing, moves that--despite initial resistance--it eventually made.

Josh Wolfson, an analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said there is merit in a breakup or sale of assets by Barrick, supported by a share price that is trading at a discount to the sum of the company's parts. "Various scenarios could be pursued to realize this value, including multiple break-up options with or without additional asset divestitures," he said.

If Barrick plans to focus on its North American assets, a breakup of the company and possible asset sales would make sense, said Anita Soni, an analyst at CIBC Capital Markets, who adds that the company's management should focus on operations that offer the best ounces of production and on favorable jurisdictions.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1255ET

data.title: India's top solar state regulator blocks 3.2 GW coal power plan, citing clean energy push
Bulgaria arrests 35 in large-scale trafficking of cultural goods
data.description:
(Reuters) -Bulgaria has arrested 35 people linked to a criminal network involved in large-scale trafficking of cultural goods across Europe, BTA news agency reported on Thursday, quoting the head of the country's anti-organised crime unit.

"We have conducted 131 searches. Within 24 hours, 35 people were detained, we found thousands of cultural valuables, with the numbers still increasing, and over 50 antique firearms," the head of the General Directorate for Combating Organised Crime (GDBOP), Boyan Raev, told a news briefing, according to BTA.

It quoted him as saying an international raids were carried out on Thursday, led by Bulgaria and coordinated with the Europol and Eurojust agencies, during which cars, safes and other items were seized.

The criminal network had been operating for over 16 years in Western Europe, the Balkans, the United States and elsewhere, Sofia Deputy City Prosecutor Angel Kanev told the media briefing.

"Over one billion dollars have been identified in the money laundering case so far," he said.

The investigation began after 2020 house raid in Bulgaria in which police seized about 7,000 cultural artefacts with inestimable historical and monetary value, Europol said in a statement.

(Reporting by Ivana Sekularac; editing by Mark Heinrich)

data.title: Soy futures slide as traders worry China may miss US buying target
Goldman Sachs raises December 2025 copper forecast
Trump administration aims to ease legal hurdles for government layoffs
data.description:
WASHINGTON (Reuters) -The Trump administration is considering options to enhance its ability to close government offices and end programs it no longer wants, according to a proposal seen by Reuters.

The administration is developing a rule to more easily manage the legal consequences of layoffs, according to the documents. Agencies would be able to lay off staff without considering performance or length of service when closing entire government programs or offices under the proposal.

The Trump administration, since January, has effectively shuttered government programs by dismissing staff, including teams that enforce civil rights laws. Federal workers have accused the administration of ignoring the required rankings when dismissing the employees. This proposal would address that issue going forward and align the government's layoff rules more closely with the private sector.

The Trump administration believes it should not have to rank employees based on performance or length of service when closing entire offices, an administration official said, adding that it does not make sense to do so. 

(Reporting by Courtney Rozen and Sarah N. Lynch in WashingtonEditing by Rod Nickel)

By Courtney Rozen and Sarah N. Lynch

data.title: North Sea crude-WTI Midland, Forties rise
Much-delayed US jobs report shows September hiring beat forecasts
data.description:
STORY: A much-delayed U.S. jobs report showed hiring in September beat expectations, but the unemployment rate ticked up.

:: 119,000

The report showed U.S. non-farm payrolls grew by 119,000 for that month, higher than the average forecast of 50,000 jobs according to economists polled by Reuters.

:: 4.4%

The unemployment rate increased one-tenth of a percentage point to 4.4%.

Thursday's report had been due on October 3, but was delayed by the record, 43-day government shutdown. 

That shutdown forced the government's Bureau of Labor Statistics to cancel the release of an October jobs report, as no data was collected for the agency's household survey.

:: -911,000

Heading into the economic data blackout, the BLS had estimated that about 911,000 fewer jobs were created in the 12 months through March than previously reported.

:: File

The delayed and canceled releases of closely-watched measures of U.S. economic strength come amid rising tests of the job market.

:: File

The growth of artificial intelligence is eroding demand for labor. Most of that pain is landing on entry-level positions, locking recent college graduates out of work. 

Despite the overall job growth, some sectors are shedding jobs including transportation and warehousing.

And some economists said the murky picture and lack of data could influence the Federal Reserve's December policy meeting. 

Fed officials will not have November's jobs report in hand after its release date was pushed back to the middle of the following month.

data.title: Amazon to Build $3B Ai Data Center in Mississippi
data.description: Amazon intends to invest at least $3 billion in a new data center campus for cloud computing and artificial intelligence in Warren County, Mississippi, Governor Tate Reeves announced on Thursday. The project is anticipated to create at least 200 direct jobs in addition to over 300 additional full-time positions in the region and secure thousands of jobs in construction and the supply chain, as part of a larger initiative to invest at least $10 billion and create 1,000 direct jobs to establish data center campuses in Madison County.

data.title: Morgan Stanley drops call for December Fed rate cut after strong jobs data
Gold falls as strong US jobs data dims prospects for December rate cut
data.description:
(Reuters) -Gold prices fell on Thursday as investors digested the September U.S. jobs report, which showed stronger-than-expected employment figures and dampened prospects for a December rate cut. 

Spot gold fell 0.7% to $4,051.89 per ounce, by 12:15 p.m. ET (1715 GMT). U.S. gold futures for December delivery fell 0.8% to $4,051.90 per ounce.    

The U.S. dollar firmed against most major currencies, making greenback-priced gold more expensive for overseas buyers. [USD/]

The closely-watched Labor Department report, delayed due to the government shutdown, showed that September nonfarm payrolls increased by 119,000, more than double the estimated 50,000 gain.

"This (data) essentially confirms what the Fed discussed in October -- a slowing yet stable jobs market. A December rate cut now appears increasingly unlikely," adding pressure to gold, said Peter Grant, vice president and senior metals strategist at Zaner Metals.

Traders now see nearly a 40% chance for a rate cut next month. Gold, a non-yielding asset, tends to do well in low-interest-rate environments.

Due to the shutdown, the Bureau of Labor Statistics has cancelled the release of the October report, instead combining it with November's figures. The combined report will be released on December 16, after the Fed's next meeting.

Meanwhile, minutes from the Fed's October meeting revealed on Wednesday that policymakers lowered interest rates despite warning that the move could heighten the risk of inflation and undermine public confidence in the central bank.

Gold, a traditional safe haven, has risen 55% this year, hitting a record high of $4,381.22 on October 20.

Despite recent consolidation, UBS raised its 2026 mid-year gold target price by $300 to $4,500 per ounce, on expectations of U.S. rate cuts, persistent geopolitical risks, and strong central bank and exchange-traded fund demand. 

Among other metals, spot silver fell 1.9% to $50.34 per ounce, platinum fell 2.4% to $1,509.10 and palladium fell 0.1% to $1,378.45.

(Reporting by Pablo Sinha in Bengaluru; Editing by Alexander Smith and Alan Barona)

By Pablo Sinha

data.title: Siemens Energy Shares Rise on Buyback -- -2-
data.description:
1027 ET - Black Sea agricultural consultancy SovEcon lifts its outlook for the Russian wheat crop by 800,000 metric tons to 88.6 million tons. Behind the increased production is an uptick in wheat coming from Siberia, with other regions also contributing. But problems remain in the southern growing region, the firm says. "Most of the recent crop gains came from regions far from export ports, while the South--Russia's main export hub--delivered another weak harvest, which remains a challenge for the 2025/26 campaign," says Andrey Sizov of SovEcon. CBOT wheat falls 0.7%, while corn is down 0.6% and soybeans slip 0.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Prices Close Lower, Tracking Weaker Soybean Oil Prices -- Market Talk

1007 GMT - Palm oil prices ended lower, tracking weakness in soybean oil coupled with expectations of weaker demand in coming weeks, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Expectations of demand remain quite sluggish and in line with seasonal trends, he adds. Ng sees support for CPO futures at 4,100 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,280 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for January delivery closed 73 ringgit lower at 4,153 ringgit a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

--

Gold Slips on Stronger Dollar, Lower December Rate-Cut Expectations -- Market Talk

0859 GMT - Gold prices slip in early trading on a stronger dollar and as investors scale back expectations of a December interest-rate cut by the Federal Reserve. Futures in New York are down 0.8% to $4,052.80 a troy ounce, while the U.S. dollar index--which tracks the greenback against a basket of other major currencies--is flat at 100.21. The Fed's minutes from the October meeting revealed many were opposed to further rate cuts next month, with traders now pricing in only a 33.8% chance, according to the FedWatch tool. "Gold faces headwinds from steady policy and a stronger dollar, which saw its biggest jump since September," says Soojin Kim from MUFG. Market watchers now await the release of September nonfarm payroll numbers for more cues into labor market trends. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Oil Rises as Traders Weigh Geopolitical Developments, Mixed Demand Signals -- Market Talk

0841 GMT - Oil prices rise in early trading after settling 2% lower in the previous session as investors weigh risks to Russian supplies and a mixed U.S. inventory report. Brent crude and WTI both rise 0.5% to $63.83 and $59.56 a barrel, respectively. President Trump sent a Pentagon delegation to Kyiv in a bid to revive negotiations aimed at ending the war in Ukraine, easing supply disruption concerns as a resolution could lead to the lifting of sanctions on Russian crude. Meanwhile, U.S. crude stocks fell by 3.4 million barrels against expectations of a 100,000-barrel rise, while gasoline inventories fell on lower weekly consumption. "Going forward, markets will focus on how sanctions reshape trade flows, whether supply growth continues to outpace demand, and the extent to which geopolitical risks inject fresh volatility into prices," says MUFG's Soojin Kim. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Prices Could Likely Rise Toward MYR4,500/ton -- Market Talk

0722 GMT - Palm oil prices are expected to stay firm in December and potentially rise toward MYR4,500/ton, Malaysian Palm Oil Council says. Stable import demand ahead of the Lunar New Year and Ramadan should support palm oil prices. Also, strong July-October output points to a sharper seasonal production decline as trees enter their biological resting phase, it says. Monsoon conditions from mid-November are also likely to disrupt harvesting, it adds. Indonesia's policy uncertainty, particularly potential adjustments to export duties and the timing of a shift to a B45 or B50 biodiesel mandate, is also a key factor that could affect exportable supplies in 2026, MPOC adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

--

Lithium Prices Get Sentiment Boost, but Supply Risks Linger -- Market Talk

0342 GMT - Lithium prices are getting a tailwind from a turnaround in market sentiment but continue to face risks from a potential resurgence in supply, says BMI, a unit of Fitch Solutions. In a report, BMI says it now expects average 2025 prices for Chinese lithium carbonate at $10,100/metric ton and for Chinese lithium hydroxide at $9,700/ton, both higher than previously envisaged. It didn't include earlier forecasts. Year-to-date averages to Nov. 18 are $10,015/ton and $9,582/ton, respectively, it says. Energy transition-related demand and Chinese supply curtailments are "sustaining an optimistic narrative, supporting price recovery," BMI says. "That said, we highlight that downside risks to lithium price strength are still present, as anticipated supply restarts threaten to stall gains, possibly before year-end." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

--

Palm Oil Falls on Lower Soybean Oil -- Market Talk

0300 GMT - Palm oil falls in early Asian trade, tracking weaker soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade overnight, says Abdul Hameed, director of sales at Pakistan-based Manzoor Trading. The edible oils often move in tandem due to their usage in similar products. However, prices could be lifted by the coming festive-driven demand, he says. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for February delivery is lower by 47 ringgit at 4,179 ringgit a ton. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

--

Iron Ore Falls as China Property Downturn Continues to Weigh on Demand -- Market Talk

0245 GMT - Iron ore falls as a persistent downturn in the Chinese property sector continues to weigh on demand. New construction starts are set to fall for the fifth consecutive year in 2025, Commonwealth Bank of Australia analyst Vivek Dhar says in a note. "With property construction accounting for 30% of China's steel demand, steel and iron ore prices are the most exposed to weaker new construction starts," Dhar says. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.7% lower at 785.50 yuan a ton. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

--

Australian Gold-Stocks Bull Even More Positive as Price Seen Rising Higher -- Market Talk

0129 GMT - UBS raises its targets on Australia's gold stocks after upgrading its metal-price forecasts for the next few years. The bank is now forecasting a gold-price peak next year of US$4,750/oz, up from US$3,900/oz previously. That "is A$7,300/oz for our Aussie miners," UBS analysts say in a note. They upgrade gold miners' estimated earnings for the next three years by 30-60%, and lift price targets by 5%-14% as a result. They say UBS's top picks are unchanged, highlighting buy ratings on Northern Star, Perseus Mining, Genesis Minerals, Ramelius Resources, Regis Resources, Vault Minerals and Bellevue Gold. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

--

Base Metals Rise as Selling Pressure Stabilizes -- Market Talk

0112 GMT - Base metals rise in Asian trading. Recent selling pressures appear to have stabilized, say Sucden Financial analysts in a note. Despite a stronger U.S. dollar, sentiment is tracking equity markets, with key technical levels in major indices still intact, the team writes. Copper was likely also bolstered by improved sentiment on a report from Chile's copper agency Cochilco, which is now expecting an improved price environment, says ANZ Research. The three-month copper contract on the LME is up 0.5% at $10,802.50 a ton, aluminum is up 0.7% and zinc is 0.5% higher. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)


Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1233ET

data.title: Siemens Energy Shares Rise on Buyback -- Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

--Brent crude oil is down 0.6% to $63.12 a barrel.

--European benchmark gas is down 1.5% to 30.70 euros a megawatt-hour.

--Gold futures are down 0.8% to $4,050.40 a troy ounce.

--LME three-month copper futures are down 0.8% to $10,712 a metric ton


TOP STORY:

Siemens Energy Shares Rise on Buyback

Shares in Siemens Energy rose after the German energy company said it plans to buy back up to 6 billion euros ($6.92 billion) of its own shares over the next three years.

Siemens' announcement came a week after it upgraded its financial targets for its fiscal 2028, signaling confidence it can deliver on the new targets while freeing up cash along the way.

Shares climbed 5.9% in early afternoon European trading Thursday to 115.20 euros and are currently trading at a record high.


OTHER STORIES:

Tailwater Capital Sees Opportunity in Natural-Gas Bottlenecks

Tailwater Capital looks to capitalize on rising demand for natural gas by backing operators of infrastructure that helps alleviate the bottlenecks hindering both producers and consumers of the fuel.

"We are constructive on the dynamics of natural gas for the foreseeable future, and we are finding deal flow that is allowing us to solve the infrastructure problems that are cropping up," said Edward Herring, a managing partner at Dallas-based Tailwater. The private-equity firm specializes in energy infrastructure investments.

--

First Mining to Sell Ontario Gold Project to Oronova Energy, Securing Stake in the Buyer

First Mining Gold is bringing in a partner to help advance work on its Cameron gold project in Ontario as part of a deal that will see it securing a major stake in Oronova Energy, and bring it alongside a team of miner explorers, developers and operators who have launched several companies around the world.

First Mining said Thursday it entered an agreement to sell a wholly-owned subsidiary that owns the Cameron project to Oronova for roughly 27 million Canadian dollars ($19.2 million). The price includes $5 million in cash, 80 million Oronova shares, and a future cash payment of at least $2 million.

--

Exxon Mobil Taking 40% Stake in Enterprise Products Bahia Pipeline

Enterprise Products Partners has agreed to sell a 40% undivided joint interest in its Bahia natural-gas-liquids pipeline to Exxon Mobil in a deal that will expand and extend the project.

Enterprise on Thursday said Exxon Mobil will contribute its proportionate share of Bahia project costs to date, or about $650 million, in a deal slated to close by early 2026.

--

U.S. Corn Export Sales Strong in Delayed Report

Export sales of U.S. corn were over 2 million metric tons for the week ended Oct. 2, which was delayed due to the government shutdown last month.

In its report published Thursday, the Department of Agriculture said that corn export sales across the 2025/26 and 2026/27 marketing years totaled 2.26 million tons for the week. Wheat sales for the week totaled 887,900 tons, and soybean sales were 924,400 tons.

--

Canada Producer Prices Continue to Climb in October, Raw Material Costs Up Again

OTTAWA--Producer prices in Canada continued to increase in October as the extended demand for safe-haven investments buoyed precious metals, while Canadian companies for a second month running paid more for raw materials.

Statistics Canada's industrial product price index increased 1.5%, a fifth consecutive monthly increase. On a 12-month basis, the producer-price index advanced 6.0%, marking a 13th straight year-over-year rise in prices for products manufactured in Canada.

--

Cracker Barrel Investors Back CEO After Logo Fiasco, But Drop Director

Cracker Barrel shareholders voted to retain the company's embattled chief executive after a calamitous rebranding campaign, but chose to dump one of the chain's board members.

In a binding vote Thursday, Cracker Barrel shareholders voted a majority of shares in favor of keeping CEO Julie Felss Masino on its board, according to preliminary results. Board member Gilbert Dávila, a longtime marketing and diversity specialist, didn't receive the necessary votes to stay on the board.

--

Second Fire at Ford Aluminum Supplier Threatens Production

A second fire broke out Thursday morning at a key aluminum supplier to Ford and other automakers. A Sept. 16 fire at the plant reduced production of F-150 pickups and other models. Thursday's fire appears to be in the same area of the plant, said people familiar with the matter.

All workers were evacuated from the Oswego, N.Y., plant, Novelis said in a statement. County fire officials said multiple fire departments remained on the scene late Thursday morning.

--

Aris Mining Buying Outstanding Stake in Soto Norte Project in Colombia for $80 Million

Aris Mining struck a deal to buy the outstanding interest in a Colombia gold development project for $80 million, setting it on a path to develop the asset and its target of producing 1 million ounces of gold a year.

The Canadian company, which already operates two underground mines in Colombia, said Thursday it has signed a binding term sheet to buy the remaining 49% interest in the Soto Norte venture from MDC Industry, known as Mubadala.


MARKET TALKS:

Live Cattle Futures Sink -- Market Talk

1103 ET - Live cattle futures on the CME are down 1.3% in morning trade, bringing the price to just above $2.14 a pound. This makes it the lowest that cattle has traded at since early July, as the sentiment around cattle prices remains "depressed," says StoneX in a note. The firm adds that boxed beef prices are also lower, and cash cattle prices look to be lower than this time last week. If cattle ends lower, it will be the third consecutive day that cattle has settled negative, ahead of tomorrow's Cattle on Feed report from the USDA. Lean hogs are up 1.1% in morning trade. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

SovEcon Raises Outlook for Russian Wheat Output -- Market Talk

1027 ET - Black Sea agricultural consultancy SovEcon lifts its outlook for the Russian wheat crop by 800,000 metric tons to 88.6 million tons. Behind the increased production is an uptick in wheat coming from Siberia, with other regions also contributing. But problems remain in the southern growing region, the firm says. "Most of the recent crop gains came from regions far from export ports, while the South--Russia's main export hub--delivered another weak harvest, which remains a challenge for the 2025/26 campaign," says Andrey Sizov of SovEcon. CBOT wheat falls 0.7%, while corn is down 0.6% and soybeans slip 0.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Oil Futures Higher in Early U.S. Trade -- Market Talk

0900 ET - Crude futures are higher with December WTI up 0.8% at $59.89 a barrel ahead of today's expiration. The market remains rangebound with bearish views of supply and demand countered by geopolitical risk. "We still see limited possibility of the U.S. crude benchmark straying far from the $60 mark through the rest of this month and into next," Ritterbusch says in a note. A firm expiration appears to be supported by yesterday's EIA report of a 3.4 million barrel decline in U.S. crude stocks, including a reduction at Cushing, the Nymex delivery hub, the firm adds. "A renewed weakening in WTI structure could prove limited short of some major geopolitical bearish headlines." Brent is up 0.8% at $64.01 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

European Gas Prices Rise on Colder Weather Prospects -- Market Talk

1606 GMT - European natural-gas prices rise, with the benchmark Dutch TTF contract up 0.7% to 31.16 euros a megawatt hour. Prices settled lower in the previous session, pressured by easing geopolitical risks amid efforts to restart talks to end the war in Ukraine. However, "colder weather has led to gas storage falling at a faster pace over the last few days," ING analysts say. "EU gas storage is now 81% full, down from a peak of just over 83% in mid-October." Meanwhile, investment funds cut their net long position and increased their short position to record highs. This leaves the market susceptible to a price surge if there are any supply issues or colder-than-expected weather, as shorts might need to cover their positions, according to the firm. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Gold Flat After U.S. Job Growth Defies Expectations -- Market Talk

1452 GMT - Gold prices are broadly flat, with New York futures trading at $4,081.20 a troy ounce after the latest U.S. data showed job growth defied expectations in September. Payrolls rose by 119,000 in the month, well above the gain of 50,000 jobs expected by economists, while the unemployment rate rose slightly to 4.4%. Meanwhile, traders are now pricing in a nearly 42% chance of a December interest-rate cut, according to the FedWatch tool. "The dollar and Treasury yields strengthened following the latest FOMC minutes, which signaled limited appetite for a December rate cut," analysts at Saxo say. However, "bullion remains in consolidation mode after rising more than 50% this year, supported by a broad mix of structural and macro drivers ahead of 2026." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

U.S. Natural Gas Inventories Decline As Expected -- Market Talk

1052 ET - U.S. natural gas storage fell last week in line with expectations as a cold snap in the eastern U.S. raised heating demand. Inventories were down by 14 billion cubic feet at 3,946 Bcf as of Nov. 14, which reduced the surplus over the five-year average to 146 Bcf from 172 Bcf the week before, the EIA reports. Stocks were 23 Bcf below the year-earlier level. The storage draw, the first of the incipient heating season, matched the average estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. Nymex natural gas is off 0.6% at $4.522/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

SovEcon Raises Outlook for Russian Wheat Output -- Market Talk

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

11-20-25 1233ET

data.title: Nestlé announces the retirement of Sanjay Bahadur
Bankinter announces early voluntary redemption of 350 mln euro securities
Nestle : Nestlé announces the retirement of Sanjay Bahadur

data.title: Siemens Energy to Reward Shareholders with Billions After Years of Lean Times
data.description:
The energy technology group Siemens Energy plans to reward its shareholders following the end of several challenging years.

The company announced on Thursday, during its Capital Markets Day in the United States, that it intends to allocate up to ten billion euros to investors by 2028. This amount will be achieved through a share buyback program worth up to six billion euros, as well as dividend payments. The DAX-listed group is benefiting from rising global electricity demand thanks to its gas turbines and grid technologies. Last week, when presenting its 2025 financial results, Siemens Energy stated its intention to pay a dividend of 70 euro cents per share for the fiscal year--marking the first payout after a four-year hiatus.

MANAGEMENT FOCUSES ON ONSHORE WIND ENERGY BUSINESS

Share buybacks can push share prices higher, as the company's value is distributed among fewer outstanding shares. On Thursday, Siemens Energy's stock price temporarily surged by over eight percent, reaching its highest level since the company's spin-off from former parent Siemens in 2020.

Siemens Energy also aims to increase its profits in the coming years. Last week, management raised its medium-term targets. CEO Christian Bruch reaffirmed that the group intends to continue growing, particularly in the gas turbines, power grids, and services segments. Siemens Energy plans to invest around six billion euros in total from 2026 to 2028. The struggling onshore wind energy business will be closely scrutinized, but Bruch indicated that now is not the time for decisions. He also remained cautious regarding acquisitions despite booming business, stating that no major takeovers are currently planned, though smaller deals in grid operations or supply chains are possible.

(Reporting by Tom Käckenhoff, Christoph Steitz. Edited by Ralf Banser. For any inquiries, please contact our editorial team at Berlin.newsroom@thomsonreuters.com (for politics and economics) or frankfurt.newsroom@thomsonreuters.com (for companies and markets).

data.title: SBI, AllianceBernstein to launch Japan's first active ETF venture, Nikkei says
Solventum Corporation entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Acera Surgical, Inc. for $850 million.
data.description:
Solventum Corporation (NYSE:SOLV) entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Acera Surgical, Inc. for $850 million on November 20, 2025. Consideration is comprised of $725 million in cash plus up to $125 million in contingent cash payments based on the achievement of certain future milestones. Morgan Stanley & Co.

LLC is serving as financial advisor to Solventum and McDermott Will & Schulte LLP is acting as legal advisor. Truist Securities is serving as financial advisor to Acera and Hogan Lovells US LLP is acting as legal advisor.

data.title: TSX Loses All Early Gains, Falls 200 Points at Midday
US Equity Indexes Lose Steam in Midday Trading
Fed's Hammack Says Rate Cuts May Boost Risks, Prolong Inflation
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland President Beth Hammack warned that lowering interest rates to support the labor market could extend the period of above-target inflation and increase financial stability risks. She said, "lowering interest rates to support the labor market risks prolonging this period of elevated inflation, and it could also encourage risk-taking in financial markets," during a conference Thursday hosted by the Cleveland Fed.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Eiffage wins second EUR 68 mln contract for Madrid Street Circuit infrastructure
FTSE 100 up but mixed US jobs data tempers gains
data.description:
(Alliance News) - The FTSE 100 snapped a five-day losing streak on Thursday, although gains were pared after mixed US jobs data pointed to interest rates staying on hold across the pond.

The FTSE 100 index closed up 20.24 points, 0.2%, at 9,527.65. It had earlier traded as high as 9,593.83.

The FTSE 250 ended 27.89 points lower, 0.1%, at 21,384.35, while the AIM All-Share rose 0.52 points, 0.1%, to 741.20.

The Cboe UK 100 was up 0.3% at 953.10, the Cboe UK 250 was 0.5% lower at 18,482.08, and the Cboe Small Companies advanced 0.1% to 17,578.62.

Keenly awaited jobs data in the US contained mixed news for the US Federal Reserve ahead of its December meeting.

The US labour market added more jobs than expected in September, according to the data published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 119,000 in September, topping the FXStreet-cited forecast of 50,000.

But figures for July and August were revised downwards by a total 33,000, while the unemployment rate edged up to 4.4% in September, from 4.3% in August. It had been expected to remain at 4.3%.

ING thinks the "mixed messages on US jobs will keep the Fed hawkish," especially given the lack of data ahead of the December 10 meeting.

Sterling was quoted at USD1.3091 at the time of the London equities close on Thursday, higher compared to USD1.3076 on Wednesday.

The euro stood at USD1.1534, slightly lower against USD1.1536. Against the yen, the dollar was trading higher at JPY157.46, compared to JPY156.67.

In European equities on Thursday, the CAC 40 in Paris rose 0.3%, while the DAX40 in Frankfurt firmed 0.5%.

In New York, markets were higher at the time of the London equity market close.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 0.3%, the S&P 500 index was 0.6% higher, as was the Nasdaq Composite.

The yield on the US 10-year Treasury was at 4.10%, trimmed from 4.12% on Wednesday. The yield on the US 30-year Treasury was at 4.72%, narrowed from 4.74%.

Nvidia rose 0.7%, easing from earlier highs, after better-than-expected third-quarter earnings and outlook, soothing some investor worries about the artificial intelligence bubble.

"There’s been a lot of talk about an AI bubble. From our vantage point, we see something very different," Chief Executive Jensen Huang said, noting sales of Blackwell chips are "off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out."

"Nvidia management's tone and outlook along with the beat and raise results should help to inject confidence back into the AI narrative again", commented HSBC analyst Frank Lee in a research note.

Back in London, Games Workshop and Halma were the day's big winners.

Games Workshop stormed 13% higher after a better-than-expected trading update.

The Nottingham, England-based fantasy game figurine maker and retailer expects pretax profit in the six months ending November 30, the first half of its financial 2026, will be at least GBP135 million, up 6.5% from GBP126.8 million a year before.

Core revenue will be at least GBP310 million, up 15% from GBP269.4 million a year before, while licensing revenue will be at least GBP16 million, down 47% from GBP30.1 million.

Jefferies analyst Andrew Wade called it an "outstanding" trading performance with core revenue growth well ahead of his 1.5% growth projection.

"This is an impressive performance from Games Workshop with another period of double-digit core revenue growth - well ahead of our expectations - adding further evidence to our view that concerns on the product release cycle are overdone," he added.

Halma leapt 9.2% as it raised guidance after making "excellent progress" in the first half of its financial year with growth broadly spread across all sectors and regions.

The Amersham, England-based safety products manufacturer now expects mid-teens organic constant currency revenue growth for the full year, upped from its previous aim of a low double-digit rise.

Citi analyst Avinash Mundhra thinks consensus forecasts could rise 3% to 5% on the back of what he called "encouraging progress."

BAE Systems rose 1.8%, recouping some of Wednesday's falls.

Defence stocks fell on Wednesday on news that the US has a new 28-point peace plan for ending the Russian-Ukrainian war.

But JPMorgan thinks the reaction is "unjustified" and provides a "compelling entry point into the sector."

The broker doesn't think the plan will be acceptable to Ukraine or its European allies, and said that even if imposed by the US it "would amount to a de facto victory for Russia, driving European defence spending even higher than planned and at a much faster pace."

JD Sports was a prominent faller, down 3.9%, as it tempered its profit outlook for the financial year.

The Manchester, England-based sportswear retailer anticipates full-year pretax profit before adjusting items to be within the lower end of current market expectations of GBP853 million to GBP888 million.

This would be down from GBP923 million in financial 2025.

JD Sports said recent indicators have shown "incrementally weaker macroeconomic and consumer external data points in our key markets."

On the FTSE 250, CMC Markets soared up 29% as it raised annual guidance reflecting accelerating momentum, record client cash balances, rising activity levels and stronger performance metrics.

The London-based trading platform now expects full-year net operating income to be around 10% ahead of current market expectations, which it put at GBP353.9 million.

But Paypoint plunged 21% as it reported a decrease in its interim profit.

The firm said it is dealing with disruption to its parcels network from the harmonisation of InPost and Yodel services and slower-than-expected growth at Obconnect.

Gold traded lower at USD4,058.47 an ounce on Thursday against USD4,081.23 on Wednesday.

Brent oil was quoted higher at USD63.44 a barrel at the time of the London equities close on Thursday, from USD63.37 late Wednesday.

The biggest risers on the FTSE 100 were Games Workshop, up 2,170.00 pence at 18,260.00p, Halma, up 306.00p at 3,618.00p, Airtel Africa, up 10.00p at 305.50p, Diploma, up 110.00p at 5,320.00p and Polar Capital Technology Trust, up 8.50p at 457.00p.

The biggest fallers on the FTSE 100 were WPP, down 14.20p at 299.40p, JD Sports, down 3.12p at 77.28p, Fresnillo, down 72.00p at 2,338.00p, Vodafone, down 1.96p at 89.82p and DCC, down 92.00p at 4,850.00p.

Friday's global economic calendar has UK retail sales, public sector net borrowing and a flash composite PMI reading.

Elsewhere, retail sales figures are due in Canada and the Michigan consumer sentiment index in the US.

Friday's UK corporate calendar has half-year results from aerospace, defence and nuclear engineering company Babcock International and full-year results from online fashion retailer Asos.

By Jeremy Cutler, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to newsroom@alliancenews.com
